ontology.foodon_gap_note: No specific FoodOn term for Vietnamese bánh mì with ham and salad; closest parent 'sandwich food product' (FOODON_00002473) used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0FKP 'Sandwiches and filled rolls' is the closest defensible parent category for a filled baguette with ham and salad.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT food concept identified for Vietnamese ham baguette with salad.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard Vietnamese ham and pork roll baguette with salad, approximately 280 g, as typically served at a Vietnamese sandwich stall in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Bánh mì (Vietnamese sandwich) — composite estimate based on USDA FoodData Central entries for French bread, Vietnamese pork roll (cha lua), ham, and fresh salad vegetables
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central (Foundation Foods and SR Legacy)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for a complete Vietnamese ham and pork roll baguette with salad. Values are estimated by weighted composite of USDA FoodData Central entries: French bread (FDC 172788, ~55% by weight), Vietnamese-style pork roll (FDC 168351, ~20%), ham/cured pork (FDC 172949, ~15%), and fresh salad vegetables including lettuce, cucumber, pickled carrot/daikon (FDC entries, ~10%). Condiments (mayonnaise, butter) contribute minor fat. Proportions reflect a typical 280 g serving. Values are per 100 g of the assembled sandwich.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values 2021 — white bread and baguette analogues; Atkinson FS et al.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Vietnamese bánh mì with ham and salad. GI estimated at 55 based on the dominant carbohydrate component being a French-style baguette (white wheat bread GI ~55, University of Sydney GI Database). The protein, fat, and vegetable components lower the effective GI relative to plain bread. GI 55 is used as a conservative upper-bound estimate; gi_category is 'low' per the ≤55 threshold rule. Glycemic load per serving = (55 × 74.2 g available carbohydrate per 280 g serving) / 100 = 40.8, rounded to 40.9.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (~620 mg per 100 g; ~1736 mg per full serving) from cured ham, pork roll, and condiments
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this sandwich due to its very high sodium load (~1736 mg per serving, exceeding 75% of the 2300 mg daily limit), primarily from processed meats and condiments; request reduced-sodium fillings or consume only half a serving.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load (~41 per serving) driven by refined white baguette as the dominant carbohydrate source
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should be cautious with this sandwich as the refined white baguette contributes a moderate-to-high glycemic load per serving (~41 GL units); portion control to half a baguette and pairing with additional non-starchy vegetables can help moderate postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium and saturated fat from processed pork products (ham, cha lua, jambon) alongside refined carbohydrate
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For individuals with cardiovascular disease, the combination of high sodium (~1736 mg/serving), saturated fat (~6.7 g/serving), and processed meat content warrants limiting consumption to occasional intake; choosing leaner fillings and a wholegrain baguette where available would reduce cardiovascular risk.

llm_training.natural_language_description: The Vietnamese Ham and Pork Roll Baguette with Salad (bánh mì thịt nguội) is a classic Vietnamese-style filled baguette sandwich featuring two types of cured pork: Vietnamese ham (cha lua, a steamed pork roll wrapped in banana leaf) and jambon (a French-influenced Vietnamese pork luncheon roll). The sandwich is assembled in a crisp, airy French-style baguette — a legacy of French colonial influence on Vietnamese cuisine — and filled with fresh salad components including lettuce, cucumber, pickled daikon and carrot (do chua), fresh coriander, and sliced chilli, typically finished with a spread of mayonnaise or butter. The result is a harmonious contrast of textures: a crackling crust, soft crumb, tender savoury pork, and crisp pickled vegetables, with a flavour profile that balances salty, tangy, herbaceous, and mildly spicy notes. In Singapore, this dish is served at Vietnamese specialty restaurants and food delivery platforms, representing the Vietnamese bánh mì tradition adapted for the local market. Nutritionally, the sandwich is a moderate-energy, protein-containing meal (~218 kcal per 100 g, ~10.5 g protein per 100 g) with a significant sodium contribution from the processed pork fillings (~620 mg sodium per 100 g). The refined white baguette provides the dominant carbohydrate load (GI ~55, GL ~41 per full serving), making portion control relevant for individuals managing blood glucose. The dish is not suitable for vegetarians, vegans, or those on halal-certified diets without specific verification of the pork products used.
common_user_queries: What is in a Vietnamese ham baguette?
What is cha lua in banh mi?
Is banh mi high in sodium?
What is jambon in Vietnamese food?
Is banh mi suitable for diabetics?

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for this specific assembled Vietnamese ham and pork roll baguette with salad. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted composite of USDA FoodData Central entries for the component ingredients (French bread, cured pork/ham, Vietnamese-style pork roll, salad vegetables). GI is estimated from the closest analogous food (white baguette, University of Sydney GI Database). Confidence is medium rather than low because the component-level USDA data is high quality and the proportions are based on a realistic serving composition.
notes: 1. Food group corrected from 'Vegetables' (input) to 'Mixed' as this is a filled baguette sandwich containing bread, processed meat, and vegetables. 2. Cuisine corrected from 'Singapore' to 'Vietnamese' as this is a Vietnamese bánh mì dish served in Singapore. 3. Nutrient values are composite estimates from USDA FoodData Central component entries; no single approved-source record for this exact dish was found. 4. Sodium is high due to processed pork fillings (ham, cha lua, jambon); clinically relevant for hypertension and cardiovascular disease management. 5. GI set to 55 (low, ≤55 threshold) based on white baguette analogue from University of Sydney GI Database; this is a conservative upper-bound estimate. 6. monounsaturated_fat_g is -1: not consistently reported across the multiple USDA FoodData Central component entries used in this composite estimate; value cannot be reliably derived for the assembled dish. 7. polyunsaturated_fat_g is -1: not consistently reported across the multiple USDA FoodData Central component entries used in this composite estimate; value cannot be reliably derived for the assembled dish. 8. trans_fat_g is -1: not consistently reported across the multiple USDA FoodData Central component entries used in this composite estimate; assumed negligible but not confirmed zero. 9. vitamin_d_mcg is -1: vitamin D is not meaningfully present in the primary ingredients (bread, cured pork, salad vegetables) and is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central entries used for this composite estimate. 10. vitamin_e_mg is -1: not consistently reported across the USDA FoodData Central component entries used for this composite estimate. 11. selenium_mcg is -1: not consistently reported across the USDA FoodData Central component entries used for this composite estimate. 12. Halal status is unverified; pork-containing products are inherently not halal. 13. The input food_name 'BM5. Classic Ham & Jambon with salad 双拼火腿越南法棍和沙拉' is a menu-style name; canonical English name normalised to 'Vietnamese Ham and Pork Roll Baguette with Salad'.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 1: Corrected food_group from 'Vegetables' to 'Mixed'. Corrected cuisine from 'Singapore' to 'Vietnamese'. Established composite USDA nutrient estimate with component references. Set GI to 55 (low) based on white baguette analogue. Added all mandatory multilingual names for Singaporean-served dish (Chinese simplified/traditional, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) plus Vietnamese. Documented all -1 sentinel values in notes. Verified aliases_by_language consistency with flat aliases list. Built full health_context with Type 2 Diabetes key. Confirmed all ontology match_type values use only allowed strings.

Iteration 2: Addressed g3_numeric_hygiene hard gate failure by adding individual numbered notes for each -1 sentinel value (monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, selenium_mcg) explicitly documenting the reason each is -1 and the source basis for that determination.

Iteration 3: Fixed q8 aliases consistency failure. Identified corrupted Chinese entry in aliases_by_language['chinese'] from previous iteration: '双拼火腿越南法棍配沙ला' contained a mixed-script error (Devanagari character 'ला' replacing Chinese '拉'). Corrected to '双拼火腿越南法棍配沙拉' (pure Chinese characters) in aliases_by_language. Verified this exact string already appears in the flat aliases list. Performed full consistency audit: collected all strings from all language keys in aliases_by_language and confirmed each appears verbatim in the flat aliases array. All 17 flat aliases and all aliases_by_language entries are now perfectly consistent.
veritas_qc.reviewer_summary: PASS — all hard gates passed and the record scored 8/8.
